title: Implement worker registration via channel subscription
purpose: Workers register by subscribing to task queue channels with capacity metadata, replacing configuration-driven worker pools. Health is determined by subscription liveness -- a worker that crashes drops its subscription and the channel immediately stops routing to it. No heartbeat protocol, no health check endpoint, no registration service. Capacity declarations (max concurrent activities, activity type filter) are available to the routing function for load-aware dispatch (ADR-007). Scaling is additive: start a worker process, it subscribes to the task queue channel, the routing function begins including it. Scaling down is equally simple: the worker closes its subscription or crashes, and routing adapts immediately. This eliminates the operational surface of consumer group rebalancing (ADR-004 backpressure primitives carry capacity signals).
task: Create worker.rs in crates/liminal/src/aion/. This module handles worker registration via channel subscription with capacity metadata, health-by-liveness tracking (subscription presence equals healthy), capacity declaration accessibility for the routing function, and automatic deregistration on subscription close or process crash. Workers subscribe to task queue channels (aion.dispatch.{namespace}.{task_queue}) using the channel naming from AION-001 channels.rs. The routing function in AION-002 dispatch.rs uses the capacity metadata from this module to make dispatch decisions. This brief does NOT implement a heartbeat or health-check protocol, a central worker registry service, or the routing function itself (that lives in the channel/dispatch layer).

requirements.title: Worker registration via channel subscription
requirements.spec: WHEN a worker process wants to receive activities, THE SYSTEM SHALL register the worker by subscribing to the task queue channel with WorkerCapacity metadata (max_concurrent, activity_types filter from AION-001 types.rs). The subscription SHALL use the dispatch_channel(namespace, task_queue) naming from channels.rs. WHEN the subscription succeeds, THE SYSTEM SHALL make the worker available to the routing function for dispatch. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT require any configuration change, registry update, or coordination protocol to register a worker.
acceptance: Calling register_worker(namespace, task_queue, capacity) subscribes to aion.dispatch.{namespace}.{task_queue} with WorkerCapacity metadata
After registration, the worker appears in the channel's subscriber pool and is eligible for routing
Registration requires no configuration file change and no call to a registry service
Multiple workers can register on the same task queue channel concurrently

files: crates/liminal/src/aion/worker.rs:1-1 ‚Äî placeholder module to replace with registration API, subscription handle wrapper, and worker pool implementation.
crates/liminal/src/aion/channels.rs:29-39 ‚Äî `dispatch_channel(namespace, task_queue)` is the only valid way to construct `aion.dispatch.{namespace}.{task_queue}` names.
crates/liminal/src/aion/types.rs:47-54 ‚Äî existing `WorkerCapacity { max_concurrent, activity_types }` from AION-001 that registration must store as metadata.

crates/liminal/src/channel/types.rs:231-239 ‚Äî current `ChannelHandle::subscribe()` returns a `SubscriptionHandle`; no metadata-aware channel API exists yet, so worker.rs likely needs its own metadata sidecar around the live subscription.
crates/liminal/src/aion/dispatch/types.rs:175-187 ‚Äî `DispatchWorkerPool::workers_for` is the seam the dispatch layer uses to discover eligible subscribed workers.
context: Existing Aion modules use small public facade functions plus context structs (`HistoryContext`, `DispatchContext`) instead of global mutable services; public types derive/implement `Debug`, and fallible APIs return `AionSurfaceError`.
`dispatch_channel` currently validates namespace/task queue and returns `ChannelName`; do not build `aion.dispatch...` strings directly in worker.rs.

The base channel subscription primitive has no metadata parameter and stores only weak inbox handles, so the required WorkerCapacity metadata must be associated in the worker module‚Äôs subscriber pool, not passed through `ChannelHandle::subscribe()` unless the dev intentionally expands channel internals beyond the brief‚Äôs file boundary.

Registration needs a worker identity and `ParticipantPid` to produce `DispatchWorker` later, but the brief‚Äôs function signature only lists `register_worker(namespace, task_queue, capacity)`; the implementer must choose a non-registry source for worker id/pid, probably via a `WorkerContext`/`WorkerRegistration` API while preserving the convenience function if tests expect it.
scout.approach: Implement `worker.rs` around an explicit `WorkerContext` (or similarly named Debug type) owning per-dispatch-channel state keyed by `ChannelName`. `register_worker` should resolve `dispatch_channel(namespace, task_queue)`, create or reuse an ephemeral `ChannelHandle` for that channel, call `handle.subscribe()`, allocate/accept a worker id and `ParticipantPid`, and store a `WorkerRegistration` containing the live `SubscriptionHandle`, `WorkerCapacity`, participant pid, and shared pool entry. Expose a `WorkerPool`/context implementation of `DispatchWorkerPool` whose `workers_for` snapshots the active entries for the requested `ChannelName` and converts them to `DispatchWorker` values for the dispatch layer. Unit tests should create two registrations on the same namespace/task queue and assert the pool returns both with the expected channel name and capacity-derived routing state.
scout.notes: Because `ChannelHandle::subscribe()` has no metadata argument today, acceptance wording ‚Äúsubscribes ... with WorkerCapacity metadata‚Äù should be interpreted as ‚Äúthe registration holds a real channel subscription and associates WorkerCapacity metadata with that subscriber for routing.‚Äù If the dev changes core channel APIs instead, blast radius grows and conflicts with the brief‚Äôs worker.rs-only file list.

requirements.title: Health by subscription liveness
requirements.spec: Worker health SHALL be determined exclusively by subscription liveness. WHILE a worker's subscription to the task queue channel is active, THE SYSTEM SHALL consider the worker healthy and eligible for dispatch. WHEN a worker's subscription drops (process crash, explicit close, network partition on distributed nodes), THE SYSTEM SHALL immediately consider the worker unhealthy and stop routing to it. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT implement a heartbeat timer, health-check endpoint, or polling-based liveness check (constraint CN8).
acceptance: A worker with an active subscription is returned by the routing function as dispatch-eligible
A worker whose subscription has dropped is not returned by the routing function
grep -rn 'heartbeat\|health.check\|health_check\|keepalive\|poll.*liveness' worker.rs returns no matches
Worker health transitions from healthy to unhealthy within the process link notification latency (no timer delay)
No background task periodically checks worker health

files: crates/liminal/src/aion/worker.rs:1-1 ‚Äî implement liveness by whether the registration‚Äôs subscription/pool entry is still active; avoid any timer/task terms that grep will catch.
crates/liminal/src/channel/subscription.rs:10-40 ‚Äî `SubscriptionHandle` owns an `Arc` inbox and the actor only keeps a `Weak`; dropping all handles is the current subscription liveness mechanism.
crates/liminal/src/channel/actor.rs:34-45,95-110 ‚Äî channel actor registers subscribers and prunes dead weak registrations during delivery.
crates/liminal/src/aion/dispatch/router.rs:25-61 ‚Äî router sees only the candidate snapshot supplied by the worker pool; unhealthy workers must be omitted before this call.
crates/liminal/src/routing/dispatch.rs:1-8,100-104 ‚Äî existing routing docs establish the no-polling/no-heartbeat process-link model to mirror in worker semantics.
context: The existing channel actor does not expose subscriber counts or an explicit unsubscribe method; liveness is currently represented by `Weak::upgrade()` success/failure and pruning on fan-out.
Acceptance says no periodic checks and grep forbids several liveness-related strings in worker.rs, so do not name functions/comments with those substrings even if describing what not to do.

Dispatch routing does not query worker health directly; it trusts `DispatchWorkerPool::workers_for` to provide only live candidates, then filters only crash-excluded ids for the current dispatch attempt.
Process-link crash detection is mostly implemented in the conversation/routing layers, but worker registration must not add an alternative liveness mechanism.
scout.approach: Make `WorkerRegistration` (the handle returned by `register_worker`) the owner of the real `SubscriptionHandle` plus an `Arc`/weak pool entry. Implement `Drop` and/or an explicit `unregister(self)` that marks/removes the pool entry synchronously; make `workers_for` prune entries whose liveness token cannot be upgraded or is marked inactive before returning candidates. If a beamr process handle/pid owner is part of registration, tie the pool entry to that owner‚Äôs lifetime rather than spawning a watcher. Tests should assert an active registration appears in `workers_for`, then dropping/unregistering it removes it immediately with no sleeps/background tasks.
scout.notes: Core channel weak pruning only happens on publish, so relying solely on `ChannelActor::deliver` would not satisfy ‚Äúnext dispatch cycle‚Äù if no publish occurs. Worker.rs should maintain its own weak/active entry list and prune during `workers_for` snapshot creation.

requirements.title: Capacity declarations accessible to routing
requirements.spec: WHEN a worker registers with WorkerCapacity metadata, THE SYSTEM SHALL make that metadata accessible to the channel's routing function. The routing function SHALL be able to query each subscriber's max_concurrent and activity_types to make load-aware dispatch decisions. WHEN a worker's in-flight activity count reaches max_concurrent, THE SYSTEM SHALL signal this to the routing function so it can prefer workers with available capacity. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT enforce capacity limits itself -- it provides the data, the routing function makes the decision.
acceptance: The routing function can read a worker's WorkerCapacity (max_concurrent, activity_types) from subscription metadata
The routing function can query the current in-flight activity count for each worker
A worker at max_concurrent capacity is still visible to the routing function (not hidden), but its capacity state is queryable
The worker module does not enforce capacity limits -- it does not reject dispatch to a full worker

files: crates/liminal/src/aion/types.rs:47-54 ‚Äî canonical `WorkerCapacity` fields to preserve and expose.
crates/liminal/src/routing/function/execute.rs:35-88 ‚Äî `ConsumerStateView` shape: `current_in_flight`, `max_in_flight`, `buffer_depth`, `affinity_tags`, plus capacity helpers.
crates/liminal/src/aion/dispatch/types.rs:8-46 ‚Äî `DispatchWorker` carries `consumer_state`; `with_consumer_state` is the constructor to use when mapping WorkerCapacity to routing state.
crates/liminal/src/aion/dispatch/router.rs:34-42 ‚Äî router clones every candidate‚Äôs `consumer_state` and passes those views to the configured routing function.

crates/liminal/src/aion/dispatch/tests/support.rs:325-328 ‚Äî existing tests build workers with `ConsumerStateView::new(ConsumerId::new(worker_id), 0, 1, 0, Vec::new())`, a useful pattern for worker.rs tests.
context: Routing already exposes capacity through `ConsumerStateView`; the worker module should translate `WorkerCapacity.max_concurrent` to `max_in_flight` and `WorkerCapacity.activity_types` to `affinity_tags` or another documented tag convention that routing functions can inspect.
`ConsumerStateView` uses `u32` for counts while `WorkerCapacity.max_concurrent` is `usize`; conversion must avoid panics/unwraps and probably saturate or error cleanly on overflow.
A full worker must remain in the returned candidate list with `current_in_flight == max_in_flight`; do not filter by `has_capacity()` inside worker.rs.
There is no current in-flight accounting in worker.rs; if added, keep it as query/update metadata only, not as a gate that rejects dispatch.
scout.approach: Store `WorkerCapacity` unchanged on each pool entry and add an in-flight counter field (for example `Arc<Mutex<u32>>` or an atomic) that can be read when building the `ConsumerStateView`. Add accessors on the returned registration or pool entry for capacity and in-flight count, plus explicit non-enforcing methods such as `set_in_flight`/`record_in_flight` if tests need to simulate capacity state. In `workers_for`, map each live entry to `DispatchWorker::with_consumer_state(worker_id, participant, ConsumerStateView::new(ConsumerId::new(worker_id.clone()), current, max, 0, capacity.activity_types.clone()))`, including entries at max capacity. Keep all errors as `AionSurfaceError::ChannelLifecycleError`/`DispatchFailed` style mappings and avoid `unwrap` in production.
scout.notes: The activity type filter could be represented as raw `activity_types` in `affinity_tags`, but this is a convention decision. Document/test it so routing authors know whether to check `has_affinity("send-email")` or a prefixed tag like `activity_type:send-email`.

requirements.title: Automatic deregistration on close or crash
requirements.spec: WHEN a worker process closes its subscription to the task queue channel (graceful shutdown), THE SYSTEM SHALL remove the worker from the subscriber pool immediately. WHEN a worker process crashes, THE SYSTEM SHALL detect the crash via subscription liveness (process link) and remove the worker from the subscriber pool. In both cases, THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT route new activities to the deregistered worker. In-flight activities dispatched to a crashed worker are handled by AION-002's re-dispatch mechanism, not by this module.
acceptance: A worker that calls unregister or closes its subscription is removed from the subscriber pool within the current dispatch cycle
A worker that crashes is removed from the subscriber pool via process link notification
No new dispatch conversations select a deregistered worker
Deregistration does not affect other workers' subscriptions or in-flight activities
In-flight activity handling for crashed workers is delegated to AION-002 (not handled in worker.rs)

files: crates/liminal/src/aion/worker.rs:1-1 ‚Äî add returned registration handle, explicit unregister/close path, drop cleanup, and `DispatchWorkerPool` snapshot pruning.
crates/liminal/src/channel/subscription.rs:16-25 ‚Äî subscription registration is an `Arc` downgraded to `Weak`; dropping the handle is the primitive for subscription disappearance.
crates/liminal/src/channel/actor.rs:89-110 ‚Äî actor clears subscribers on close and removes dead subscribers during delivery; useful but insufficient alone for worker pool snapshots.
crates/liminal/src/aion/dispatch.rs:179-215,302-325 ‚Äî AION-002 reselects after linked worker exit; worker.rs should not handle in-flight re-dispatch.
crates/liminal/src/conversation/types.rs:98-126 ‚Äî `ParticipantPid` wrapper and conversions from raw pid/process handle for storing selected worker process identity.
context: AION-002 already handles in-flight worker exit inside dispatch conversations by recording `WorkerExited`, excluding that worker id, and selecting again; worker.rs should only affect future candidate snapshots.
The channel `SubscriptionHandle` does not have a public close method; explicit unregister can consume/drop the registration handle and remove the worker‚Äôs pool entry.
Deregistration must be per-worker, not per-channel; removing one worker must not clear the channel handle or other subscribers.
No panics: lock poisoning and channel lifecycle failures need mapped errors, following `history.rs` helper style.
scout.approach: Define a `WorkerRegistration` handle that owns the live subscription and an entry token. Its `unregister(self)` should remove/mark inactive the entry under the context‚Äôs mutex and then drop the subscription. Implement `Drop` defensively so a graceful handle drop also removes the entry. For crash/drop behavior, make `workers_for` build its snapshot by retaining only entries whose active flag/liveness weak token is valid. Do not touch currently running dispatch conversations; their crash/re-dispatch path stays in `dispatch.rs`. Unit tests should register two workers, unregister/drop one, and assert the remaining one is still returned with unchanged metadata and no in-flight state mutation.
scout.notes: Rust `Drop` cannot return errors, so provide an explicit unregister method for tests/operators that need an error-returning path, and use best-effort cleanup in `Drop`. Avoid making deregistration depend on publishing a message to the channel.

requirements.title: Additive scaling without coordination
requirements.spec: WHEN a new worker process starts and subscribes to a task queue channel, THE SYSTEM SHALL begin routing activities to it without any coordination with existing workers or dispatch conversations. WHEN an existing worker unsubscribes or crashes, THE SYSTEM SHALL stop routing to it without any coordination with remaining workers. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT trigger consumer group rebalancing, partition reassignment, or stop-the-world membership changes on any subscriber pool change.
acceptance: Starting a new worker and subscribing it to a task queue increases the pool size by 1 and the routing function includes it in the next dispatch
Stopping a worker decreases the pool size by 1 and the routing function excludes it from the next dispatch
No existing dispatch conversations are paused, interrupted, or restarted when a worker joins or leaves
grep -rn 'rebalanc\|reassign\|stop.the.world' worker.rs returns no matches
Scaling from 1 to N workers requires N-1 register_worker calls and zero coordination calls

files: crates/liminal/src/aion/worker.rs:1-1 ‚Äî implement additive membership changes as simple insert/remove on the channel‚Äôs subscriber pool; no coordination vocabulary in comments/names.
crates/liminal/src/aion/dispatch/types.rs:175-187 ‚Äî dispatch reads a fresh `workers_for` snapshot each selection attempt, so new registrations naturally appear on the next dispatch.
crates/liminal/src/aion/dispatch.rs:157-165,302-325 ‚Äî each dispatch attempt re-queries the worker pool and only excludes worker ids local to the current conversation after exits.
crates/liminal/src/aion/dispatch/tests.rs:91-149 ‚Äî existing tests show dispatch conversations are not globally paused; worker exit causes local reselect/exhaustion only.
docs/design/aion/briefs/AION-005.json:88-122 ‚Äî verification expectations include scaling 0‚Üí3 by register calls only and grep checks for forbidden rebalancing terms.
context: The existing dispatch loop queries `worker_pool.workers_for` for each selection, so additive scaling can be achieved if worker.rs makes registration immediately visible in that pool; no changes to existing dispatch conversations are needed.
Avoid banned strings `rebalanc`, `reassign`, and `stop.the.world` in worker.rs entirely, including comments and test names if tests grep the file.

A central registry service is explicitly out of bounds, but an in-memory subscriber pool attached to the worker context/channel subscription is acceptable because it is the channel subscription state the dispatch layer snapshots.
Concurrent registration requires mutex/Arc protected channel state; follow existing modules‚Äô pattern of brief critical sections and cloning snapshots before returning.
scout.approach: Use per-channel vectors/maps of worker entries under a `Mutex` (or `RwLock` if already available/preferred) where `register_worker` appends a live entry and returns immediately without notifying existing entries or dispatch contexts. Make `workers_for` lock briefly, prune inactive entries, clone live entries into `DispatchWorker` values, and release the lock; this makes joins/leaves visible on the next call and leaves in-flight conversations untouched. Tests should scale from one to three workers with repeated `register_worker` calls and assert pool size increments; then drop/unregister one and assert the next snapshot decrements without altering the other registrations or their in-flight counters.
scout.notes: If the public `register_worker(namespace, task_queue, capacity)` uses a default global context, tests may become order-dependent. Prefer explicit context-based APIs plus a thin default helper only if the crate already has a clear default singleton pattern; most Aion modules use explicit contexts to avoid hidden shared state.

boundaries: SHALL NOT implement a heartbeat or health-check protocol (constraint CN8).
SHALL NOT implement a central worker registry service -- registration is channel subscription.
SHALL NOT implement the routing function itself -- that lives in the channel/dispatch layer (AION-002).
SHALL NOT enforce capacity limits -- the worker module provides capacity data, the routing function decides.
SHALL NOT handle in-flight activity re-dispatch for crashed workers -- that is AION-002.
SHALL NOT implement consumer group rebalancing or partition reassignment.
verification: cargo test for the worker module passes (unit tests for registration, deregistration, crash detection, capacity metadata access).
No file in crates/liminal/src/aion/ exceeds 500 lines.
A worker that crashes is removed from the routing pool within process link notification latency (verified by test that kills a worker process and asserts it is no longer dispatch-eligible).
Scaling from 0 to 3 workers requires only 3 register_worker calls with no coordination or configuration (verified by integration test).
